channel.title: Comments for page "Video Killed The Radio Star"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Video Killed The Radio Star"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excuse the necropsy, but I thought of something I don’t think a lot have thought about, and it connects the two 1548 iterations (and this tale too).

Essentially, the last line “I don’t want to go.” can also be inferred as being destroyed by the entities mentioned in the new 1548 (the ones trying to invade the solar system) like all the other stars are being destroyed as well. Just something I thought of and maybe it’ll make it a little more interesting, but ofc it doesn’t have to be (bc there is no established canon ofc)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea why I never corrected this back when I first read the other comment (and he was right, an earlier version was correct and I'm not sure why I changed it in the first place).

Regardless: Thanks for reminding me. Feexed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded - the author seems to be confusing neutron stars with white dwarfs, or the Chandrasekhar Limit with the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) Limit.
A white dwarf has a mass limit of ~1.4 solar masses (Chandrasekhar limit). A neutron star has a mass limit of ~2.3-2.4 solar masses currently (TOV limit), though increasing massive ones are being found.

Good tale, but this needs correcting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bit of a nitpick, but since it's heavily dependent on the science, I feel obligated to point this out. (Not to mention I'm pretty sure an earlier version was correct). 1.4 solar masses is a bit below the Chandrasekhar Limit, the mass at which electron degeneracy pressure cannot prevent a white dwarf from collapsing into a white dwarf. Neutron stars pretty much never form with a mass lower than that, because otherwise there isn't enough sustained pressure for it. The upper limit for neutron degeneracy pressure is ~2.27 solar masses, about 50% higher. A more realistic mass for 1548 would be 2.3 or 2.4 solar masses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never liked the Hateful Star, and I have never liked meta stuff. Competently executed, but since it hit two of my downvote buttons, I have to -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"if it ever shifts opposite the direction of its motion the whole thing comes crashing down in a planck second."

Ok… then why did it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last line reminds me not of Spider-Man's death in Avengers: Infinity War, but the last thing the 10th Doctor said before he regenerated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a video about the Hateful Star…

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rfQ3gqquG-A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Dr. Thompson, I'm not feeling so good."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I don't want to go."

Oh, snap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is nothing if not a respectful sendoff for a beloved article and — if I may be so bold — a way of dealing with the trauma of its deletion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To clarify: It's (probably) a dick move to rewrite the author's stuff if they intentionally deleted it.

It is most certainly not a dick move to write a story dealing with that deletion (from an in-universe perspective). Particularly if the story itself doesn't engage in any drama or hostility (and this one doesn't, as far as I can see!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not really against the rule per say. The site does it out of good faith even though the site can totally do it if they want due to the license.

The site just doesn't want to have another Fishmonger incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sent a link to Pincier as soon as I wrote it, and I need to do something to prop up the other two tales I have on the site about this SCP. I haven't got a reply yet but I don't think he'd be bothered by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They can't archive because it goes against the author's decision. It wasn't deleted because of downvotes, the author quit and took all his work with him. It would be against the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really do hope they archived the Hateful Star.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sure but i guess it just kinda seems rude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"No memory of having starred
Atones for later disregard
Or keeps the end from being hard."

(From 'Provide, Provide', by Robert Frost)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this information is accurate, I could be wrong, but I remember seeing in the subreddit that he left due to the drama that is happening on the comunity right now, mostly caused by the new logo. I could be wrong though.
Also, since you mentioned… Dread it, Run from it, downvotes still arrive…
When I'm done, half of the Skips will still exist, perfectly balanced, as all things should be. I hope they -ARC you…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why did they decide to delete it?

Also, totally knew someone was going to reference Infinity War.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never got the chance to read the Hateful Star, since I'm reading all Skips in numerical order, but it's sad that the author decided to delete it. Wish I had read Series 1 faster, but I guess I'll never see it. Sad. And that last line… I don't feel so good; Mr. O5, I don't wanna go!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, the hateful star has been…removed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, that ending line hit hard.

Godspeed, you glorious ball of hate. ;-;7 +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very, very, very well done. Nothing I can see wrong with this — exactly the epilogue the Hateful Star needed and deserved. +1 with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
claps before turning away as a single tear falls from face

This was the send-off that both we and that fucking star needed. Upvoted to hell and back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't believe I'm going to miss a star.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that ending line. Even though it was hateful, it felt a new emotion: fear. Goodbye, shining star, you will always be a part of my canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Letting people delete their stuff is more of a courtesy- a creative commons license can't be retracted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last line's a real kicker. I love how it's tied into real modern theories too.
I'll miss you, hateful star.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so momentary hesitation would be its end, huh. interesting. there are a lot possibilities on why it left the equilibrium maintained by its extreme speed, but in the end we only observed the result…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would it be too much to ask to replace all references in tales in such to the old 1548 to this one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing about the tale popped out at me, but the exposition was smooth and the last line tugged at the heart strings. Hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh fuck. D: That last line. If I wasn't already broken up about this… ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but are we allowed to write tales about skips who's authors intentionally removed them from the wiki?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1.4 assumes a uniform density as a sphere. The reason why rotation keeps certain overmassive neutron starts from collapsing is that the centrifigul force of rotation alters the shape and density. The incredible motion of this object was enough to fiddle with the density and prevent a collapse as well. But if it ever stopped, that's the ballgame. You can read more about blitzars here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blitzar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really understand. How could the motion prolong its collapse? And why suddenly it couldn't? It sounds a bit haphazard of an excuse for me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little something devoted to what was one of my favorite SCPs on the site. It wasn't perfect but it was one of the first I read, and really jived with my love of astronomy. Gonna miss it.

Also I think this is the 3rd tale I've written about it. Jesus.

Image source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Crab_nebula_pulsar.jpg
